sections.content:

Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 13 of about 72 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I see objects at the bottom:
- id=108: Black object, 41 pixels, rows 55-61, cols 8-14 (left panel border)
- id=109: Black object, 41 pixels, rows 55-61, cols 18-24 (right panel border)
- id=110: Yellow object, 6 pixels, rows 55-56, cols 20-22 (yellow indicator on right panel)
- id=111: Yellow object, 6 pixels, rows 57-59, cols 8-9 (yellow indicator on left panel)
- id=112: White pixel at (58, 11) (button on left panel)
- id=113: White pixel at (58, 13) (button on left panel)
- id=114: White pixel at (58, 21) (button on right panel)
- id=115: White pixel at (60, 21) (another white pixel)
So the buttons are:
- Left panel: W at cols 11, 13
- Right panel: W at col 21
I've been clicking the B cells on the gray/blue panels, but the W cells are the actual buttons! Let me reconsider.
Looking at the row 58:
BBBBBBBgYYBWBWBgBbBBBWBBBbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBB
- Left panel (cols 7-15): gYYBWBWBgB
  - B cells at cols 10, 12, 14
  - W cells at cols 11, 13
- Right panel (cols 17-25): bBBBWBBBb
  - B cells at cols 18-20, 22-24
  - W cell at col 21
So the left panel has 3 B cells with 2 W cells between them, and the right panel has 6 B cells with 1 W cell in the middle.
I've been clicking the B cells, but maybe I should be clicking the W cells?
Wait, but earlier I found that clicking the W cell at col 21 on the right panel (blue panel) produced up-right by 8 movement. So the W cells are also interactive.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the W cells are the actual buttons, and the B cells are just decorative or part of the panel border.
Looking at the left panel:
- gYYBWBWBgB
  - g: gray border
  - YY: yellow indicator
  - B: black cell
  - W: white button
  - B: black cell
  - W: white button
  - B: black cell
  - g: gray border
  - B: black cell (maybe part of the border)
So the left panel has 2 W buttons and 3 B cells (which might also be buttons).
The right panel has:
- bBBBWBBBb
  - b: blue border
  - BBB: 3 black cells
  - W: white button
  - BBB: 3 black cells
  - b: blue border
So the right panel has 1 W button and 6 B cells (which might also be buttons).
I've found that:
- Clicking B cells on the left panel (gray): moves pattern down-left by 8 or left by 16
- Clicking W cell on the right panel (blue): moves pattern up-right by 8
- Clicking B cells on the right panel (gray): moves pattern up-right by 8
- Clicking B cells on the left panel (blue): no gameplay change
So the interactive buttons depend on which panel is gray vs. blue. The gray panel's B cells are interactive, and the blue panel's W cell is interactive.
Now, let me think about the goal. The pattern alternates between (8, 14) and (16, 6). The yellow object id=45 is at rows 24-27, cols 45-48.
Maybe the goal is to get the pattern to a position where it overlaps with the yellow object when the panels are aligned. But the pattern can only be at (8, 14) or (16, 6), which doesn't match (24, 45).
Wait, maybe I need to look at the yellow indicators on the panels. The left panel has a yellow indicator at cols 8-9 (id=111), and the right panel has a yellow indicator at cols 20-22 (id=110).
Maybe these indicators show which button to click? Or maybe they indicate the current state of the panels.
Let me try clicking the W cells on the left panel (cols 11, 13) to see what they do. Currently, the left panel is gray (with B cells at 10, 12, 14 and W cells at 11, 13).
Actually wait, looking at the current row 58:
BBBBBBBgYYBWBWBgBbBBBWBBBbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBB
The left panel is at cols 7-15: gYYBWBWBgB
- Col 7: g (gray)
- Col 8-9: YY (yellow indicator)
- Col 10: B (black)
- Col 11: W (white)
- Col 12: B (black)
- Col 13: W (white)
- Col 14: B (black)
- Col 15: g (gray)
So the left panel has B cells at 10, 12, 14 and W cells at 11, 13. The yellow indicator is at cols 8-9.
The right panel is at cols 17-25: bBBBWBBBb
- Col 17: b (blue)
- Col 18-20: BBB (black)
- Col 21: W (white)
- Col 22-24: BBB (black)
- Col 25: b (blue)
So the right panel has B cells at 18-20, 22-24 and W cell at 21.
I've been clicking the B cells, but maybe I should try clicking the W cells on the left panel (cols 11, 13) to see what they do.
Let me try clicking col 11 (W on left panel).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking the W cell at col 11 on the left panel (gray panel)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 11}])
print(f"Action result: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

# Check the new pattern position
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 14:
        col = n['boundary'][0][1]
        if col < 32:
            print(f"Pattern now at: ({n['boundary'][0][0]}, {n['boundary'][0][1]})")

# Check current panel configuration
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"\nRow 58: {lines[58]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

Pattern now at: (16, 6)

Row 58: BBBBBBBbYYBWBWBbBgBBBWBBBgBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBB
